title: Build a CRUD REST API in Laravel With Sanctum Authentication
altTitle: Laravel API From Scratch: Auth, Resources, and Clean JSON in 60 Minutes
excerpt: Build a fully authenticated CRUD REST API in Laravel using Sanctum for token auth, API Resources for clean JSON, and proper validation on every endpoint.

seo.title: Build a Laravel CRUD API With Sanctum Auth
seo.description: Step-by-step tutorial to build a REST API in Laravel with Sanctum token authentication, API Resources, FormRequest validation, and clean JSON responses.

content.text: If you've built Laravel apps with Blade views, making the jump to a pure API backend feels like starting over. The routing is the same, the models are the same, but suddenly you're thinking about tokens, JSON responses, and what to return when validation fails. This tutorial walks through the whole thing from scratch - authentication, CRUD, validation, and clean output.
We'll build a simple Product API: create, read, update, delete - protected by Sanctum token auth. By the end you'll have a template you can reuse for any resource.
Step 1: Install Sanctum

composer require laravel/sanctum
php artisan vendor:publish --provider="Laravel\Sanctum\SanctumServiceProvider"
php artisan migrate
Then add the HasApiTokens trait to your User model:
Step 2: Auth Endpoints - Register and Login
Step 3: The Product Model and Migration
php artisan make:model Product -m
Step 4: Form Request Validation

php artisan make:request StoreProductRequest
php artisan make:request UpdateProductRequest
Step 5: API Resource for Clean Output
php artisan make:resource ProductResource
Step 6: The Product Controller
Common Mistakes

items: Returning $product directly instead of through an API Resource - Eloquent's toArray exposes every column including internal fields. Always shape your output with a Resource class.
Not scoping queries to the authenticated user - without $request->user()->products(), any user can read any product. Always scope to the owner.
Skipping authorisation on show/update/destroy - just because a user is authenticated doesn't mean they own the resource. Use Policies or a manual ownership check.
Returning 200 for resource creation - use 201 Created for store and 204 No Content for destroy. Clients that check status codes will thank you.

content.text: Final Takeaway

A clean Laravel API isn't complicated - it's just a consistent set of patterns applied to every resource: FormRequest for validation, API Resource for output shaping, Sanctum for auth, and proper status codes throughout.

Action step: use this as a template for your next resource. Scaffold the model, migration, FormRequest, Resource, and Controller in one go with php artisan make:model Invoice -mcr --requests --resource and fill in the blanks.
